should_trigger: Review our proposed agent tool protocol before we announce it as a standard. It is mostly designed by committee, has no independent implementations yet, and would replace several existing plugin APIs.
We are building a new platform runtime that will replace our legacy API surface. Help decide whether we need upward compatibility, adapters, or a clean break.
Our architecture council wants to standardize a new enterprise data access language from scratch. Evaluate whether a simpler de facto interface is likely to win instead.
A vendor says their future operating environment will obsolete our current stack, but migration details are vague. Use historical system-design constraints to assess the plan.
We keep adding UI features and command syntax to a large system, but the core process, linkage, data descriptor, and runtime contracts are still unclear. What should we stop and fix first?
Assess whether hardware acceleration for this platform primitive should be standardized now or whether we should first prove the behavior in software.
should_not_trigger: Summarize the biography of John F. Sowa in two paragraphs.
Explain what TCP/IP stands for without evaluating a standards process.
Write a generic checklist for project management status meetings.
Help me format a JSON schema for a small internal config file that has no adoption, compatibility, or migration implications.
Give me a history lesson about IBM mainframes without applying it to a live architecture or standards decision.
edge_cases: We need to comply with an existing mature standard, but we are also considering extensions that might become a de facto API. Use this skill only for the extension and adoption-risk analysis.
A startup wants a clean-sheet protocol because there is no installed base yet. Use the skill lightly: focus on prototype evidence and likely de facto rivals, not legacy migration.
Our current internal API is ugly but widely used. Decide whether standardizing it as-is, clarifying it, or replacing it with a migration bridge is the least risky option.
